diagrams.description: The history of macroeconomics unfolds through seminal works and events: Adam Smith’s Wealth of Nations (1776) laid classical foundations; the Great Depression prompted Keynes’s General Theory (1936), marking the birth of modern macro. The 1950s‑60s brought the Monetarist challenge (Friedman & Schwartz), the 1970s saw Rational Expectations (Lucas critique) and stagflation, while the 1980s‑90s forged the New Keynesian synthesis. The Global Financial Crisis of 2008 spurred macroprudential regulation and a renewed focus on financial stability.
